Based on the search results, I found information about the 2026 ATP Tour calendar:

## Overview

The 2026 ATP Tour calendar comprises the Grand Slam tournaments, supervised by World Tennis, the ATP Finals, the ATP Tour Masters 1000, the ATP 500, the ATP 250, and the United Cup (organized with the WTA). Additionally, the 2026 calendar includes the Davis Cup (organized by World Tennis), Next Gen ATP Finals, and Laver Cup, none of which distribute ranking points.

## Key Details

ATP has announced the 2026 ATP Tour calendar, featuring tournaments across 29 countries, in addition to the four Grand Slam events.

The ATP Masters 1000 events include Indian Wells, Miami, Monte Carlo, Madrid, Rome, Montreal, Cincinnati, Shanghai, and Paris.

## Season Structure

The 2026 ATP season spans the global tennis stage, opening in January with the Australian Open and concluding in November after the ATP Finals. Between those milestones, the Tour moves through Roland Garros, Wimbledon, and the US Open, alongside a packed schedule of Masters 1000, ATP 500, and 250 events across five continents.

For the complete week-by-week detailed schedule, you can access the official sources:
- **ATP Tour Official Website**: atptour.com
- **ESPN Tennis Schedule**: espn.com/tennis/schedule
- **Wikipedia 2026 ATP Tour**: Contains detailed tournament tables

These sources provide the full breakdown by week and specific tournament dates for the entire 2026 season.

2026 ATP Tour

Details
- Duration: 2 January – 20 December 2026
- Edition: 57th
- Tournaments: 65
- Categories: Grand Slam (4) ATP Finals Next Gen ATP Finals ATP 1000 (9) ATP 500 (16) ATP 250 (30) Laver Cup Davis Cup United Cup Hopman Cup

Achievements (singles)
- Most titles: Jannik Sinner (5)
- Most finals: Jannik Sinner (5)
- Prize money leader: Jannik Sinner ($6,702,466)
- Points leader: Jannik Sinner (5,950)

The 2026 ATP Tour is the global elite men's professional
[tennis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tennis) circuit organized by the [Association of Tennis Professionals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_of_Tennis_Professionals) (ATP) for the 2026 tennis season. Also included in the 2026 calendar are the [Davis Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Davis_Cup) (organized by the ITF), [Next Gen ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Next_Gen_ATP_Finals), [Hopman Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hopman_Cup) and [Laver Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laver_Cup), none of which distribute ranking points. These tables present the number of
[singles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singles_(tennis)) (S), [doubles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doubles_(tennis)) (D), and [mixed doubles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mixed_doubles_(tennis)) (X) titles won by each player and each nation during the season, within all the tournament categories of the 2026 calendar: the [Grand Slam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis)) tournaments, the [ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Finals), the [ATP Masters 1000](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Masters_1000), the [ATP 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_500) tournaments, and the [ATP 250](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_250) tournaments. The players/nations are sorted by:
- Total number of titles (a doubles title won by two players representing the same nation counts as only one win for the nation);
- Cumulated importance of those titles (one Grand Slam win equalling two Masters 1000 wins, one undefeated ATP Finals win equalling one-and-a-half Masters 1000 win, one Masters 1000 win equalling two 500 events wins, one 500 event win equalling two 250 events wins);
- A singles > doubles > mixed doubles hierarchy;
- Alphabetical order (by family names for players).

The following is a list of notable players (winners of a main tour title, and/or part of the ATP rankings top 100 in singles, or top 100 in doubles, for at least one week) who announced their retirement from professional tennis, became inactive (after not playing for more than 52 weeks), or were permanently banned from playing, during the 2026 season:
[Roberto Bautista Agut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roberto_Bautista_Agut)joined the professional tour in 2005 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 9 in singles in November 2019. He won 12 career singles titles. On 16 April 2026, Bautista Agut announced he would retire from professional tennis at the end of the season. [[15]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-17) [[16]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-18) [Egor Gerasimov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egor_Gerasimov)joined the professional tour in 2010 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 64 in singles in November 2020. On 3 May 2026, Gerasimov announced his retirement from professional tennis in an Instagram post that was written in both Russian and English. [[17]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-19) [David Goffin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Goffin)joined the professional tour in 2009 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 7 in singles in November 2017. He won six career singles titles, and was a finalist at the [2017 ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017_ATP_Finals_%E2%80%93_Singles). On 26 March 2026, Goffin announced he would retire from professional tennis at the end of the season, after struggling with a long-term knee injury. [[18]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-20) [Gaël Monfils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ga%C3%ABl_Monfils)joined the professional tour in 2004 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 6 in singles in November 2016. He won 13 career singles titles. On 1 October 2025, Monfils announced that he will retire at the end of the season. [[19]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-21) [Jamie Murray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamie_Murray)joined the professional tour in 2004 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 1 in doubles in April 2016. He won 34 career doubles titles, including two [major](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis)#Tournaments)titles in men's doubles and five in mixed doubles. On 15 April 2026, Murray announced his retirement from professional tennis. [[20]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-22) [Kei Nishikori](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kei_Nishikori)joined the professional tour in 2007 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 4 in singles in March 2015, being the highest-ranked Japanese and Asian man in ATP history. He won 12 career singles titles, and was the first Japanese and Asian man to reach a major singles final in the [Open Era](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_tennis#Open_Era), at the [2014 US Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_US_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles). On 30 April 2026, Nishikori announced he will retire at the end of the season. [[21]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-23) [Milos Raonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milos_Raonic)joined the professional tour in 2008 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 3 in singles in 2016. He won eight singles titles, and was a finalist at the [2016 Wimbledon Championships](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_Wimbledon_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles). He announced his retirement on his social media pages on 11 January 2026, 18 months after his final match. [[22]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-24) [Stan Wawrinka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stan_Wawrinka)joined the professional tour in 2002 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 3 in singles in January 2014. He won sixteen career singles titles, including three major singles titles at the [2014 Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_Australian_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles), [2015 French Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2015_French_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles)and [2016 US Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_US_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles), and a [doubles gold medal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tennis_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men's_doubles)at the [2008 Summer Olympics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics). On 19 December 2025, Wawrinka announced that he will retire at the end of the season. [[23]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-25) [Bernabé Zapata Miralles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernab%C3%A9_Zapata_Miralles)joined the professional tour in 2015 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 37 in singles in May 2023. Zapata Miralles announced his retirement from professional tennis in December 2025, citing struggles with his mental health. His final tournament was the [Copa Faulconbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copa_Faulconbridge)in May 2026. [[24]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-26) [[25]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-27) [[26]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-28) [Antoine Hoang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antoine_Hoang)became inactive having not played for more than a year. [Dominik Koepfer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominik_Koepfer)became inactive having not played for more than a year. [David Marrero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Marrero)became inactive after receiving a 31 month ITIA tour ban after breaching the wildcard rules. [[27]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-29) [Lucas Pouille](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas_Pouille)became inactive having not played for more than a year. [Tomislav Brkić](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tomislav_Brki%C4%87)returned to the tour at the [Hong Kong Tennis Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_ATP_Hong_Kong_Tennis_Open)after being absent since 2023. [Andrey Golubev](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrey_Golubev)returned to the tour at the [Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Australian_Open)after being absent since 2023. [Philipp Kohlschreiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philipp_Kohlschreiber)returned for one tournament at the [2026 Upper Austria Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Upper_Austria_Open_%E2%80%93_Doubles), partnering [Joel Schwärzler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Schw%C3%A4rzler)in the doubles event. [Fabrice Martin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fabrice_Martin)returned to the tour at the [Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Australian_Open)after a year of inactivity. [Aleksandr Nedovyesov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleksandr_Nedovyesov)returned to the tour at the [Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Australian_Open)after a year of inactivity. [J. J. Wolf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J._J._Wolf)returned to the tour at the ITF M15 event in Naples, Florida after a year of inactivity. 2026 ATP Tour

Details
- Duration: 2 January – 20 December 2026
- Edition: 57th
- Tournaments: 65
- Categories: Grand Slam (4) ATP Finals Next Gen ATP Finals ATP 1000 (9) ATP 500 (16) ATP 250 (30) Laver Cup Davis Cup United Cup Hopman Cup

Achievements (singles)
- Most titles: Jannik Sinner (5)
- Most finals: Jannik Sinner (5)
- Prize money leader: Jannik Sinner ($6,702,466)
- Points leader: Jannik Sinner (5,950)

The 2026 ATP Tour is the global elite men's professional
[tennis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tennis) circuit organized by the [Association of Tennis Professionals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_of_Tennis_Professionals) (ATP) for the 2026 tennis season. [[2]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-:0-2) The 2026 ATP Tour calendar comprises the [Grand Slam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis)) tournaments, supervised by the [International Tennis Federation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Tennis_Federation) (ITF), the [ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Finals), the [ATP Tour Masters 1000](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Tour_Masters_1000), the [ATP 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Tour_500), the [ATP 250](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Tour_250), and the [United Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Cup) (organized with the [WTA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Women's_Tennis_Association)). Also included in the 2026 calendar are the [Davis Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Davis_Cup) (organized by the ITF), [Next Gen ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Next_Gen_ATP_Finals), [Hopman Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hopman_Cup) and [Laver Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laver_Cup), none of which distribute ranking points. These tables present the number of
[singles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singles_(tennis)) (S), [doubles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doubles_(tennis)) (D), and [mixed doubles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mixed_doubles_(tennis)) (X) titles won by each player and each nation during the season, within all the tournament categories of the 2026 calendar: the [Grand Slam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis)) tournaments, the [ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Finals), the [ATP Masters 1000](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_Masters_1000), the [ATP 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_500) tournaments, and the [ATP 250](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_250) tournaments. The players/nations are sorted by:
- Total number of titles (a doubles title won by two players representing the same nation counts as only one win for the nation);
- Cumulated importance of those titles (one Grand Slam win equalling two Masters 1000 wins, one undefeated ATP Finals win equalling one-and-a-half Masters 1000 win, one Masters 1000 win equalling two 500 events wins, one 500 event win equalling two 250 events wins);
- A singles > doubles > mixed doubles hierarchy;
- Alphabetical order (by family names for players).

[Roberto Bautista Agut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roberto_Bautista_Agut), [David Goffin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Goffin), [Gaël Monfils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ga%C3%ABl_Monfils), [Jamie Murray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamie_Murray) (former top 10 in doubles and former doubles world number 1), [Kei Nishikori](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kei_Nishikori), [Milos Raonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milos_Raonic), and [Stan Wawrinka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stan_Wawrinka) announced their respective retirements during the 2026 season except for Murray and Raonic, they both played their last matches in 2025 and 2024, respectively.
The following is a list of notable players (winners of a main tour title, and/or part of the ATP rankings top 100 in singles, or top 100 in doubles, for at least one week) who announced their retirement from professional tennis, became inactive (after not playing for more than 52 weeks), or were permanently banned from playing, during the 2026 season:
[Roberto Bautista Agut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roberto_Bautista_Agut)joined the professional tour in 2005 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 9 in singles in November 2019. He won 12 career singles titles. On 16 April 2026, Bautista Agut announced he would retire from professional tennis at the end of the season. [[15]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-17) [[16]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-18) [Egor Gerasimov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egor_Gerasimov)joined the professional tour in 2010 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 64 in singles in November 2020. On 3 May 2026, Gerasimov announced his retirement from professional tennis in an Instagram post that was written in both Russian and English. [[17]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-19) [David Goffin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Goffin)joined the professional tour in 2009 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 7 in singles in November 2017. He won six career singles titles, and was a finalist at the [2017 ATP Finals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017_ATP_Finals_%E2%80%93_Singles). On 26 March 2026, Goffin announced he would retire from professional tennis at the end of the season, after struggling with a long-term knee injury. [[18]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-20) [Gaël Monfils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ga%C3%ABl_Monfils)joined the professional tour in 2004 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 6 in singles in November 2016. He won 13 career singles titles. On 1 October 2025, Monfils announced that he will retire at the end of the season. [[19]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-21) [Jamie Murray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamie_Murray)joined the professional tour in 2004 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 1 in doubles in April 2016. He won 34 career doubles titles, including two [major](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis)#Tournaments)titles in men's doubles and five in mixed doubles. On 15 April 2026, Murray announced his retirement from professional tennis. [[20]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-22) [Kei Nishikori](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kei_Nishikori)joined the professional tour in 2007 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 4 in singles in March 2015, being the highest-ranked Japanese and Asian man in ATP history. He won 12 career singles titles, and was the first Japanese and Asian man to reach a major singles final in the [Open Era](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_tennis#Open_Era), at the [2014 US Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_US_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles). On 30 April 2026, Nishikori announced he will retire at the end of the season. [[21]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-23) [Milos Raonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milos_Raonic)joined the professional tour in 2008 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 3 in singles in 2016. He won eight singles titles, and was a finalist at the [2016 Wimbledon Championships](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_Wimbledon_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles). He announced his retirement on his social media pages on 11 January 2026, 18 months after his final match. [[22]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-24) [Stan Wawrinka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stan_Wawrinka)joined the professional tour in 2002 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 3 in singles in January 2014. He won sixteen career singles titles, including three major singles titles at the [2014 Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_Australian_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles), [2015 French Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2015_French_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles)and [2016 US Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_US_Open_%E2%80%93_Men's_singles), and a [doubles gold medal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tennis_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men's_doubles)at the [2008 Summer Olympics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics). On 19 December 2025, Wawrinka announced that he will retire at the end of the season. [[23]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-25) [Bernabé Zapata Miralles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernab%C3%A9_Zapata_Miralles)joined the professional tour in 2015 and reached a career-high ranking of No. 37 in singles in May 2023. Zapata Miralles announced his retirement from professional tennis in December 2025, citing struggles with his mental health. His final tournament was the [Copa Faulconbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copa_Faulconbridge)in May 2026. [[24]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-26) [[25]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-27) [[26]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-28) [Antoine Hoang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antoine_Hoang)became inactive having not played for more than a year. [Dominik Koepfer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominik_Koepfer)became inactive having not played for more than a year. [David Marrero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Marrero)became inactive after receiving a 31 month ITIA tour ban after breaching the wildcard rules. [[27]](./2026_ATP_Tour#cite_note-29) [Lucas Pouille](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas_Pouille)became inactive having not played for more than a year. [Tomislav Brkić](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tomislav_Brki%C4%87)returned to the tour at the [Hong Kong Tennis Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_ATP_Hong_Kong_Tennis_Open)after being absent since 2023. [Andrey Golubev](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrey_Golubev)returned to the tour at the [Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Australian_Open)after being absent since 2023. [Philipp Kohlschreiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philipp_Kohlschreiber)returned for one tournament at the [2026 Upper Austria Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Upper_Austria_Open_%E2%80%93_Doubles), partnering [Joel Schwärzler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Schw%C3%A4rzler)in the doubles event. [Fabrice Martin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fabrice_Martin)returned to the tour at the [Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Australian_Open)after a year of inactivity. [Aleksandr Nedovyesov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleksandr_Nedovyesov)returned to the tour at the [Australian Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Australian_Open)after a year of inactivity. content.text:
Sim, e é bem fácil — o jogo é um arquivo HTML único, sem servidor. Cada visitante joga a própria carreira no navegador dele (o save fica no navegador de cada pessoa). Opções gratuitas:

**Mais simples: Netlify Drop** — renomeie o arquivo para `index.html`, acesse drop.netlify.app e arraste o arquivo. Em segundos você ganha um link público (tipo `seujogo.netlify.app`) para compartilhar com quem quiser. Não precisa nem de conta para testar.

**Alternativas:**
- **itch.io** — plataforma de jogos indie; bom se quiser divulgar para jogadores. Sobe como "HTML5 game" num zip.
- **GitHub Pages / Vercel / Cloudflare Pages** — também gratuitos, um pouco mais burocráticos (conta + repositório), mas ótimos para manter versões atualizadas.

Duas limitações para ter em mente: o save fica preso ao navegador/dispositivo de cada jogador (o exportar/importar resolve para trocar de aparelho), e não existe interação entre jogadores — um ranking global comparando as carreiras de todos exigiria um backend (banco de dados), o que é um projeto maior.

Se quiser, preparo o pacote pronto para upload (arquivo renomeado para `index.html` + zip para o itch.io). Quer?

Based on my search results, here's comprehensive information about Google Play Console developer account requirements for 2026:

## Developer Account Fee

The Google Play Console developer account cost 2026 is a $25 USD one-time fee. Unlike Apple's $99/year fee, Google charges you once at registration and never again, regardless of how many apps you publish.

## Account Type Options

You can choose either a Personal Account (requires closed test with 12 testers) or an Organization Account (requires a D-U-N-S number).

## Closed Testing Requirements for Personal Accounts

If you have a newly created personal developer account, you must run a closed test for your app with a minimum of 12 testers who have been opted-in for at least the last 14 days continuously. 

Key details about the 12-tester requirement:

- To pass Google Play closed testing, you need a minimum of 12 testers to opt in to your closed test and remain opted in for 14 consecutive days.

- They must use real Android devices with genuine Google accounts. Emulators, bots, and duplicate accounts don't count.

- This requirement applies to all personal developer accounts created after November 13, 2023.

- Personal accounts require closed testing with 12 testers for 14 days.

## Organization Account Differences

Personal accounts do not require a D-U-N-S number. In contrast, for companies, the Google Play Console organization account D-U-N-S number required in 2026 is mandatory.

content.text:
Sim, dá — jogos HTML podem virar apps Android. Dois caminhos:

**1. PWABuilder (mais fácil)** — hospede o jogo online (Netlify etc.), adicione um manifest de PWA, e o site pwabuilder.com gera o pacote Android (.aab) pronto para enviar à Play Store. O app é basicamente o site "embrulhado".

**2. Capacitor (mais robusto)** — empacota o HTML dentro de um app nativo que funciona offline, sem depender de hospedagem. Exige instalar Android Studio e um pouco mais de trabalho técnico.

Requisitos da Play Store hoje:

- Conta de desenvolvedor Google Play: **US$ 25, taxa única**
- Contas pessoais novas precisam passar por **teste fechado com 12 testadores por 14 dias contínuos** antes de publicar (amigos/família servem, com dispositivos Android reais)
- Política de privacidade e ficha da loja (ícone, capturas de tela, descrição)

Antes de publicar, eu recomendaria adaptar a interface para celular (botões maiores, layout vertical) — o jogo hoje foi desenhado para tela de desktop, embora funcione em mobile. Isso eu posso fazer quando você decidir seguir esse caminho.

Resumindo a rota natural: site grátis agora → se fizer sucesso, PWABuilder + conta de US$ 25 → teste fechado → Play Store.

Sources: [Play Console Help — Get started](https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/6112435?hl=en), [Play Console Help — App testing requirements](https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/14151465?hl=en)
